rationale: A PowerShell process running a base64-encoded command (parent PID 4408) spawned multiple child processes that created local user accounts — including one added to the local Administrators group (EV-002: 'net user /add [id]_Admin ... & net localgroup administrators [id]_Admin /add') — and then a second encoded PowerShell session (parent PID 2588) cleaned up all three accounts (EV-003, EV-004, EV-005). The create-then-delete pattern, encoded commands loading a module from a non-standard path ('C:\[x]\invoke-[x]\Invoke-[x].psd1'), execution from the Administrator Temp directory, and logging to 'C:\[x]\atc_execution.csv' are consistent with an automated attack-simulation or adversary-emulation framework (e.g., Atomic Red Team) testing account-creation techniques. While the cleanup and simulation context reduce the likelihood of a live intrusion, the encoded parent command, non-standard module path, and confirmed local admin group manipulation (EV-002) cannot be dismissed without verifying the authorised testing scope; no change-management or authorisation artefact is present in the package to confirm this was sanctioned.
